name: Villages of the Alpujarras: Paterna, Mairena and Ugíjar
name_original: Pueblos de las Alpujarras: Paterna, Mairena y Ugíjar —  circular desde Laujar de Andarax
description: This difficult 37.7-mile road cycling route through the Alpujarras connects Paterna, Mairena, and Ugíjar, offering challenging climbs and sc
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Láujar De Andarax are characterized by mountainous terrain within the Sierra Nevada National Park and the Alpujarra of Almería. The region features diverse landscapes, including the green basin of the Andarax Valley with its orchards and vineyards, and the rocky peaks and dense pine forests of the Sierra Nevada and Sierra de Gádor. Cyclists encounter impressive elevation changes, with routes offering significant ascents on mostly paved surfaces. This area provides a range of challenging terrain for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes around Láujar De Andarax The most popular road cycling route is Villages of the Alpujarras: Paterna, Mairena and Ugíjar, a 37.7 miles (60.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 21 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers substantial climbs through historic villages. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Ugíjar – Válor Village loop from Cherín, a difficult 37.5 miles (60.4 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain and passes through varied mountain scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Láujar de Andarax – La Ragua Pass loop from Beires, a 59.6 miles (96.0 km) trail leading through the Sierra Nevada foothills and over La Ragua Pass, often completed in about 5 hours 27 minutes. Road cycling around Láujar De Andarax is defined by mountainous terrain, the fertile Andarax Valley, and extensive pine forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to very challenging routes. The routes in Láujar De Andarax are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 16 reviews. More than 1000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Láujar De Andarax's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Láujar De Andarax?
A: Road cycling in Láujar De Andarax primarily features mountainous terrain with significant elevation changes, set within the Sierra Nevada National Park and the Alpujarra of Almería. You'll find mostly paved surfaces traversing diverse landscapes, including the green Andarax Valley with its orchards and vineyards, and the rocky peaks and dense pine forests of the Sierra Nevada and Sierra de Gádor.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Láujar De Andarax?
A: While many routes in the region are challenging, there are options for varying difficulty levels. The area offers some easier rides, typically under two hours with around 500 feet of elevation gain. Out of the 24 available routes, one is classified as easy and ten as moderate, providing suitable choices for less experienced cyclists.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Experienced road cyclists will find plenty of challenging routes with substantial climbs. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20120581" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Láujar de Andarax – La Ragua Pass loop from Beires&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 59.6-mile (96.0 km) trail that leads through the Sierra Nevada foothills and over the demanding La Ragua Pass, often taking over 5 hours to complete. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38169462" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Ragua Pass – View of La Calahorra Castle loop from Cherín&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 80 km with significant elevation.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: The routes offer stunning natural beauty and historical landmarks. You can cycle past the fertile Andarax Valley, through pine and chestnut woods, and enjoy views of the Sierra Nevada and Sierra de Gádor. Notable natural features include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4397931" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Benínar Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5590550" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Small Waterfall of the Andarax River&lt;/a&gt;. For panoramic views, consider routes that pass by viewpoints like the Mirador de la Vega.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Láujar De Andarax are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24519493" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ugíjar – Válor Village loop from Cherín&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40311689" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Láujar de Andarax – Road to Benínar Reservoir loop from Laujar de Andarax&lt;/a&gt;, both offering varied scenery and challenges.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Láujar De Andarax?
A: The region is noticeably greener and cooler than the coastal plain in summer, making it a more pleasant destination during warmer months. However, the diverse landscapes and varying elevations mean that spring and autumn also offer ideal conditions with milder temperatures and vibrant scenery. Winter can be challenging due to higher elevation passes like La Ragua.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Láujar De Andarax?
A: The road cycling routes in Láujar De Andarax are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 16 reviews. Cyclists often praise the challenging mountainous terrain, the stunning views of the Sierra Nevada and Andarax Valley, and the opportunity to explore historic villages and natural features.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay along the road cycling routes?
A: Láujar de Andarax and the surrounding villages offer local gastronomy and charming places to eat, some with outdoor terraces overlooking the river. You can find traditional dishes and local wines. The recreational area of "El Nacimiento," less than a kilometer from Láujar de Andarax, also has a bar-restaurant and picnic areas, making it a good stop for refreshments.

Q: Can I find long-distance road cycling trails in this region?
A: Yes, the area offers several long-distance routes that are ideal for extended rides. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20120581" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Láujar de Andarax – La Ragua Pass loop from Beires&lt;/a&gt; is nearly 60 miles (96 km) long, providing a significant challenge and a full day's ride through the Sierra Nevada foothills.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural attractions to explore while cycling?
A: Láujar de Andarax is a historic village with a rich past. You can explore landmarks such as the ruins of the Alcazaba, the Mudejar church of San Andrés, and several 17th and 18th-century fountains, including the Baroque-style Pilar de la Plaza. The "Ruta de los Pilares" is an urban route that allows visitors to discover 16 historic fountains within the village itself. For more information on the region's history and culture, you can visit &lt;a href="https://visitingalmeria.com/towns-villages/laujar-de-andarax-guide-discover-laujar-de-andarax/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;visitingalmeria.com&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the elevation gain like on road cycling routes in Láujar De Andarax?
A: The road cycling routes in Láujar De Andarax are known for their impressive elevation changes. Many routes feature significant ascents, with some exceeding 2,000 meters of climbing over 100 kilometers. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20120581" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Láujar de Andarax – La Ragua Pass loop from Beires&lt;/a&gt; involves over 2,250 meters of elevation gain.
tips:
This beautiful town, with a long history, has a square where, if you arrive pedaling in the heat, you can cool off and recharge your batteries on the terrace of the bar.
It is worth a visit to its parish church which, given its size, is nicknamed "the cathedral of the Alpujarras".
Paterna del Río is one of those charming Almeria mountain towns. In its main square you find a bar where you can have a snack.
The stretch of road, which connects Mairena and Mecina Alfahar, surprises you with a large number of horseshoe curves. The panoramic view from the top is truly wonderful. Worth the effort.

Population geographically located in the center of the Alpujarras, and where it is one of the centers that make up this well-known area of the province of Granada. From this point you can go to a large number of surrounding towns, headed by Sierra Nevada.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a difficult road ride. It requires a very good level of fitness due to continuous ascents and descents, with a significant elevation gain of over 1,200 meters. The Alpujarra region is known for its challenging climbs.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is mountainous, featuring continuous ascents and descents through the foothills of the Sierra Nevada. Roads are generally well-paved, offering smooth tarmac, but you should be prepared for constant changes in elevation. You'll cycle through green rolling hillsides and deep valleys.

faqs.question: Where does this cycling route start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Láujar de Andarax. Parking is generally available within the town, which serves as a common starting point for routes in the area. Look for public parking areas or street parking in the vicinity of the town center.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 60.7 kilometers (37.7 miles) long. It typically takes around 3 hours and 20 minutes of active cycling time, but this can vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery or visit villages.

faqs.question: What towns and landmarks will I pass through on this route?
faqs.answer: You will cycle through several picturesque Alpujarran villages, including Paterna del Río, Mairena (passing the Mairena Hairpins), and Ugíjar. The route also begins and ends in Láujar de Andarax, a historic town with notable architecture.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Alpujarras offer excellent cycling conditions for much of the year. Early spring is particularly beautiful when almond trees are in bloom, painting the landscape pink. Autumn also provides pleasant temperatures and stunning fall colors. Summers can be very hot, making early morning rides preferable, while winters can be cold at higher elevations.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle in the Sierra Nevada Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for road cycling on public roads within the Sierra Nevada Natural Park. However, always respect local regulations, stay on marked routes, and be mindful of the natural environment and local communities.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is a road cycling path, and dogs are not typically ridden on bikes for such distances, the towns you pass through may have varying policies regarding dogs in establishments. If you plan to bring a dog and stop in villages, it's best to check individual business policies. Always keep dogs on a leash in public areas.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling or hiking paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route partially overlaps with several other paths. Notably, it covers sections of the Transandalus (41% of the route) and the Andalucía - South Route (Provincia de Granada) (11%). You'll also briefly encounter parts of the Centro BTT Nevada - Nº 3 Bocas del Aire and Sendero Río Molinos.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this challenging road cycling trip?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and elevation, it's crucial to bring plenty of water, especially during warmer months, as distances between villages can be significant. Essential items include appropriate cycling gear, nutrition, a repair kit, sun protection, and layers of clothing suitable for changing mountain weather. A GPS device with the route loaded is also highly recommended.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as you pass through villages like Paterna del Río, Mairena, and Ugíjar, you will find opportunities for cafes, restaurants, and small shops to refuel. Láujar de Andarax, your starting point, also offers various amenities.
